video_title: Claudia Sahm: "There Are Problems Under the Hood"
transcript: We don't forecast recessions, right? It's usually because they're very severe and kind of abrupt. You know, the economy is moving long and then all of a sudden it contracts or move backward. Usually something and often multiple somethings have to go wrong all at once. So I could see some seeds of a recession. Hello, welcome to Wealthon. Maggie Lake. Joining me today to discuss the outlook for the US economy and labor market is Claudia S, chief economist at New Century Adviserss and founder of SM Consulting. Hello. Wonderful to have you on. >> Hi, great to be here today. Thank you. >> Where where are you on recession? And we have to ask you because you created one of the benchmarks for the Fed that people measure. Are we in a recession? Have we had a recession? Is the US even allowed to have a recession? So I am not on re recession watch right now. >> So that uh you know there there are concerns to have about the economy. I think there's some big structural shifts potentially happening in the economy. We talked about AI. We talked about you know all of these the cost shocks the that really can rewire kind of the fundamentals of the economy sometimes in a very persistent way. that is different than a recession, which is it's a broad-based contraction economic activity, but they tend to be short-lived. They're temporary. They're not like shifts in the trend, the whole direction where we're headed. They're they're much more abrupt uh changes. And and right now, the the signs that we often have of a recession don't appear. As I said, the labor market has been stable. The unemployment rate is not >> uh rising. uh there seems to be growth is good. It it may not be it may be more focused on the buildout of artificial intelligence and the data centers but there's a lot of capital investment happening. It's not just data centers. So so broadly speaking the US economy it's expanding. It's you know moving forward. I'm so I don't put particularly high chances on a recession though I will note that not by definition but almost by re like experience we we don't forecast recessions right it's usually because they're very severe and kind of abrupt you know the economy is moving long and then all of a sudden it contracts or move backward usually something and often multiple somethings have to go wrong all at once So, I could see some seeds of a recession. You know, we have a stock market that's very highly valued. We'd have a lot of of expectations behind AI. I think those are are probably well founded over the long term, but timing is tough, >> right? And and you you could have and I think AI is really it's a it it really is like the source of optimism, right? in terms of like the economy, the growth, the capital expenditures. Um, and and that's important, right? And there are times in the past where we've had whatever was the source of our optimism got the legs knocked out from under it and that contributed to a recession. So, I'm not I'm not predicting any of this. I don't like that's not my base case. Again, I'm not on recession watch, >> but you always got to kind of pay attention to like what would it take like what what would have to go wrong and those those things are always out there. It's just, you know, do you really activate all the bad news at once. So, I I you know, don't totally rest easy, but I I have some concerns about the economy, but recession is not the main one right now. >> Not the base case right now. Well, it's such it's so interesting because we always and it's and it's great to talk to someone who's sort of in the data and and the sort of looking at the fundamentals as opposed to sort of the zeit guys finger in the air, but has it seems that so much of what's happening and what keeps consumers moving is the wealth effect. Is it more important now than it than it was when you first started looking at the economy or has it kind of always been an element? It just it just now where we get headlines about the stock market and everyone's on their phones, you know, day trading. Like is it is it more important uh are we more bound to the the fate of the stock market now as an economy? >> In terms of the if you think about the economy and GDP, consumer spending is, you know, 70% of GDP in the US. What's the most important thing for consumer spending? It's people's income, is their paychecks, right? Like that to me is still that's the fundamental piece of people have jobs they're getting paid consumption like they keep spending the economy keeps motoring along right so so I would not put wealth in terms of kind of the spending the GDP like that's not where I would put a central focus >> it is an important contributor and it can be a particularly large contributor at times where there's a lot of wealth being created Right. So in a moment like this where the stock market really is moving ahead, you have the people who are going to benefit from that directly and this is not all Americans but the ones who are invested in the stock market who are seeing either their retirement funds or their own you know personal savings getting you know bigger and you know watching those returns go up that can help encourage them. It gives them the wherewithal to do more spending if they want to. Now a lot of times people who are in the stock market already have a lot of wealth. They've been kind of spending what they wanted to spend before. So it hasn't really unlocked some some potential. The other piece about the stock market at times like this is I I do think this idea of it captures some of the optimism, the outlook, the things are going to be better and that people even not in the stock market could pick up on, right? and just be a little more forwardlooking, maybe a little less cautious with their spending. Uh, and so I I do think it the stock market is kind of sitting over the economy as a whole right now and it's giving it some of the positive. And that struck me in particular in the early months of the conflict in the Middle East where normally, you know, going into a a military conflict, gasoline prices, right? Like that normally would be enough to just pull everything down. Not just in terms of the economy, but just the overall mood and sentiment. and the stock market, the AI trade, all of that, like it was giving a counterbalance >> to that negativity that, oh, the conflict in the Middle East, that's going to be temporary. We're going to push through this, the stock market, the wealth creation, this is like, you know, the sky as far as we can see. So, I I do think that the the mindset that we have, whether we're very optimistic or pessimistic, it's important. So it's an important overlay >> but you you still have to have the money to spend or you got to have the access to the credit right and there so there are limits to it. So I don't put it as kind of the fundamental it's kind of the extra >> in terms of the growth. >> Let's start with the labor market. What is the state of the US labor market >> as we came into this year we really started to see some signs of stability. I I don't want to oversell it. I don't want to say things we've got an acceleration but but particularly in job creation which had ground to a standstill >> last year we we've seen some jobs added and and that is very encouraging and at the same time we've seen the unemployment rate >> stay steady 4.3% now for several months. So, you know, under the hood, there are some real issues with the labor market. Like, not everything is uh ideal, but overall, you look at it and are pretty pleased with that that stability that we've found, especially relative to last year where there were some real concerns about particularly job creation. H I think that there's so many concerns out in the universe about AI just coming in and sort of wiping out swaths of jobs and sectors. Are you surprised that we're not seeing more of an impact or you're are you surprised at how resilient the labor market seems to be >> in terms of AI? I am not surprised. So it is potentially a big changer technology we could see and I think we will see disruptions in the labor market from AI but it's going to take time right we have over 150 million workers tens of millions of businesses it takes time for a technology to work its way through the labor force and partic in particular the the big worries about AI is going to automate away workers we're going to have mass layoff payoffs. People are hard to replace, right? And AI is in its infancy as a technology. So, I'm not surprised that the timelines that came out in terms of everything is going to change in a year and five years, that was just too aggressive. But I just because AI hasn't reshaped the labor market yet doesn't mean that it won't in the future. So I I I think it's still a really important topic to be had and and trying to study the places in the labor market, some industries like information technology, the financial sector where AI is being deployed more broadly to really try and understand is it helping workers do their jobs better >> and if they're doing their jobs better, they also got to should be paid the fact that they're doing their jobs better or is it going to replace workers and which types of workers and making sure in terms of policy we're set up and ready to help people get retoled for that new reality. So, it's moving more slowly. We have a chance to answer those big questions. Uh but it's it's still coming and I completely understand the concerns that workers have that people have about what is coming at them. >> Yeah. But as you say, if it is moving more slowly, then at least we do have time to try to get ahead of it and try to try to problem solve around it. uh you know to at least cushion the changes that are coming which will help with that anxiety that seems to be everywhere. So what do you think is keeping what where where's the strength in the in the US labor market? Why are we seeing this stabilization and and do the quality of the jobs look good? >> The stability in the labor market comes from two features that really have been with us now for almost about two years. So you know we talked about AI is forwardlooking. There's a lot that we've seen it in the recent past that's still very important for the present. So it's often been described as the low higher low fire economy. So we'll start with the good part, the low fire, right? We have seen layoff rates that are historically very low and that is this is a really a pretty good labor market for someone who has a job and likes their job. Like those are two, you know, very important criteria, but it is a good because the layoff rates tend to be very low. Now the other part of stability which is a you know a a problematic side of stability is we have low hiring. So that means that if you're a worker who doesn't have a job this is a very difficult labor market. So people coming out of high school out of college getting back into the labor force like this is tough. Not recession level tough but tough. Like this really is hard. It's also difficult for people who have a job but want a better job or a different job for a whole host of reasons. An important one is a lot of times the way we move ahead in our careers is by switching jobs. And if you're in a in a labor market where there isn't a lot of hiring happening, there's not a lot of climbing the career ladder happening either. So it's stability, but stability isn't always good, right? And so but but it's but it's but it is better than the bottom falling out. High layoff rates, low hiring rates. Uh but it's a you know a I worry some that we could have a false sense of security >> about the labor market. And I think when you talk to workers this comes up about you know just hanging on to my job. A phrase that's come out more recent is called job hugging, which sounds a little too warm and fuzzy to me. But this idea of like you hang on to the job you have. You're not looking for the opportunities. Maybe you're not even investing in the skills that would get you into those opportunities. That can be a problem right now because you're not going to get those bigger paychecks. But it certainly can be a problem years down the road if you haven't been able to, you know, move those opportunities. So I think that's the labor market right now. It's good. is really not good enough because you want to see that movement >> but relative to other parts of the economy like inflation I think the labor market is a in a probably a better place >> yeah what you describe is not that sort of dynamic mobile labor market that we're used to in the US and kind of usually sets us apart you know where people will will move to another part of the country for a better job to get to to to kind of rise up and you lose that compounding so I can see where that is stable is not always the greatest thing. So the the the new Fed chair Kevin Worsh and the FOMC need to take all of this on board. Uh they're they're talking about making a lot of changes um looking at data that appears to not maybe be reflecting reality in the economy. Um do you agree with what you heard so far in terms of the plans? I mean there there you know we don't have a lot of details but um in in terms of plans to overhaul do you think it's needed? Do we need to update the the data collection? We can always improve on the data collection the so I agree with it in the spirit of improvement and you know the US economy is a tough one to measure right over $30 trillion economy lots of it's you know dynamic new technologies new so like measurement is hard and that's actually a good thing right because it means the economy is really dynamic that's what makes it a difficult measurement I take issue with the way Kevin Worsh kind of set up is that, you know, we're going to bring the national accounts into the modern age. And I it was a little more negative. I kind of like to frame this in we I mean the United States has the best statistical system in the world. Like we follow are on the cutting, you know, people follow the way the US does their statistics. We're on the cutting edge. So that that isn't to say we can rest on our laurels and we never have. We need to continue to innovate. So um I would you know I thought it was the framing of it. I want to be very careful that people don't say oh well you know the data we have right now they're completely useless. They're not up to date and so then I can just go tell whatever story I want to. I think fed into that though because there is a distrust of the numbers and it comes straight from the top of of you know from from Trump's from tweets from either the president himself or the white house who say like well that's not you know um there there is a distrust of the numbers that's crept into inflation in particular. >> Yes. And and that's something so it's this kind of going trying to find this sweet spot of yes the data can be better. Yes, we can improve our Yes, we must keep improving our methods to keep up with an economy that's always changing. So there that I completely affirm, but I see it running alongside sometimes this well the data aren't any good so I don't need to pay attention to the data. It's like the data we have they are not perfect but they are the best we have and they're actually really pretty good and you you need to be careful when you're using them. You need to look at lots of different data sources. I think that was something being trained as an economist at the Fed. I was there for over a decade. I often joked about the Fed as the Hoover vacuum of data. I mean, we would just go grab everything we could find, bring it in, assess its quality, kind of look at it, see what com, you know, try to tell the story with the data and and I think that's the right approach to it. And we should keep trying to improve the quality of the data and yet being very careful that we do not undermine the statistical uh agencies and particular like the the quality of our statistics comes from the ability to go out and ask people ask businesses how are you doing? What are you doing? Do it in a systematic way. do it in a way that is representative of the broad population, the broad business community. Like only statistical agencies are that careful about making sure you get the full picture. That is incredibly important particularly for policy makers. I mean the Fed needs to be making decisions that where they have an accurate sense of how it's affecting the economy as a whole, not just like one little corner or one other corner. So >> I uh I I think that'll I think there will probably be some something useful that comes out of that particular task force on the data collection. There's one other thing that I think the Fed needs to be pretty careful in that the uh statistical agencies like the Bureau of Labor Statistics, the Bureau of Economic Analysis, they're the ones that create the inflation data. They're the ones that create the unemployment, the employment data. So, in some ways, like we shouldn't be going after the referee, >> right, in terms of because we certainly don't want to get it in. Well, the way the Fed's going to do its job is by changing the data. Like, that would not be a good like that would just be bad all around. And I do not think that is his intention at all. It's just I feel like the Fed should be a little bit careful telling telling the statistical agencies how to do their job because the statistical a might look at the Fed and tell them how to do their job. >> Yeah. Yeah. stay in your lane. >> Well, this is kind of the world we're in where where everything's up for, you know, the lines of kind of demarcation have been blurred between branches and maybe between, you know, agencies. We've certainly seen uh you know that that we're in a different era for the moment anyway in Washington. Um what do you think about the the change in communication? This is the other big big issue and this is on more on on how the Fed functions rather than policy question but um no dot uh reducing the statement making it clear there there may not be frequent press conferences only if needed maybe the Fed members won't speak as often just a real attempt to sort of cut back on communication is that a positive or a negative in your mind >> cutting back on communication from the Fed would be a real negative in my opinion And it's not it's not just I want more more more of the like it they need to communicate so that people understand. So there are there are tools that the Fed has used to communicate that could be improved just like the data could be improved. Right? My concern say with this summary of economic projections often referred to as the dot plot. My concern with it is it's hard to understand. Right? let's let's improve it so people that it it doesn't create misunderstandings. It's often, you know, people take it one way, but that's not really the way it's meant. And I think that's on the Fed, right? If you create a tool, if you create kind of a way to talk to people, and they're repeatedly getting tripped up by it, go back and fix your tool. But I see it as fixing the tool as opposed to deleting the tool, right? And so that's the the and and it's communication. The Fed has actually used communication as a policy tool to help it do monetary policy. There are times where say the federal funds rate is at zero like during the pandemic or during the global financial crisis in 2008. And so they use language about what they're going to do in the future and then financial markets react to that. So there are times where the Fed actually uses its words as a tool of monetary policy. I think when they need to do that so explicitly, it's pretty narrow, right? Again, if they have the when the Fed funds rates at zero, but also over time, the Fed, and this is what I think is so important about communication is that it's a way to be transparent >> in the service of accountability. >> So the Fed is these are unelected officials. I mean they are nominated by the president, confirmed by the Senate. So they, you know, there are elected officials that are kind of helping, you know, get them in place, but and they have some really big responsibilities in terms of the decisions they make affect interest rates that affect, you know, millions of Americans and businesses, right? And so their accountability is, you know, they do report to Congress. Congress created the Fed. Congress could take the Fed away. it can change its powers, but in general, their kind of day-to-day operations. They're given the ability to just make the decisions and and not be influenced by the the politics or the politicians. But to me, like that's a pretty special responsibility. And one way to really show you're accountable is to show your work. >> Explain to people what you're doing, why you're doing it. No amount of communication can make up for bad outcomes, right? If inflation is really high, unemployment is really high, people are still rightly going to be very unhappy with the Fed. But like being clear about what you're looking at, what are you thinking about, what are your tradeoffs, what are your value judgments can go a long way to help setting up the policy situation. in particular when you think about things happen to inflation or in the labor that aren't >> within the Fed's control, right? So, but if you've had this kind of real effort >> to explain yourself, so to me like the communication should really all be centered about transparency and accountability and this question of like can we use our words to do monetary policy. I think there there are times where that is kind of like you don't have a lot of other tools. So yes, we need to understand how to do that. But in general, it's just like pull back the curtain, communicate, be straight with people. And I think that will go a long way. And I am worried that some of this we're going to talk less is going to roll back some of the gains that the Fed has made in terms of being more transparent. And I think that would be that could be a a really big problem. >> Plus, when we've seen attempts to kind of lock down information, even if it's for a very valid reason, we have leaks. I mean, then it just kind of then it becomes can you trust what you're hearing because it's getting leaked out through or is it not or is it somebody who's talking up their position? I mean, that's typically what happens in Washington, right? It's very hard to contain information. So that I wonder if that becomes a destabilizing factor for the market. >> Absolutely. And Kevin Worsh has said that he doesn't want the Fed to talk so much. So then financial markets will just go figure it out. They'll look at the data. They won't look at the Fed. They'll look at the data. Then they'll like, you know, make their bets on what interest rates uh should be. And you know, the Fed talking, it just kind of muddies the water. So, the Fed can't look at what financial markets do. Um, I mean, I I don't put nearly as high a value on reading financial markets the way the Kevin Worsh does. And I'm also trained as an economist. The idea that you withhold information and that makes markets better is just not how I like that's not how I'm trained at all. Um, but but setting all of that aside, Kevin Wars may not want to talk in public, but markets are going to fill in the blanks. They're going to be making guesses, which means things were just going to be more volatile, noisier. They're going to have to listen to other Fed officials. They'll try to piece it together the best they can in public. And then it will really ratchet up the value of getting private information. And the Fed has had a history, and this is, you know, not unique to the Fed, but I mean, their their information is really valuable. Like if you knew when the Fed was going to do something to interest rates and nobody else knew, you could make some money off of that. >> A lot of money. >> And so there have been times where the Fed, you know, Fed officials at private events at, you know, hosted by banks or which probably shouldn't be say anything. They're reading body language. They're looking at their facial expression, you know, but not everybody's in the room. >> Yes. And so like these things have happened over time and you know the Fed has ethics rules in place and you know but if if if Worsh is really serious about dialing back the public communication they will have to be ruthless about dialing back the private communication >> because there will be so much incentive for private companies to try and get that information >> and I just like that that's like the worst way and it goes it is completely on the opposite end of the spectrum about I talked about transparency and accountability and we should be serving the public. Private information for gain like this is just not is not the direction the Fed wants to go in. And frankly, if you make information scarce, you turn up the value of it and people will find a way. And uh it's just not >> especially when there are so many concerns about ethics right now in Washington. I think it's fair to say and and you know the crackdown there's there's a I hear it I see in the chats in in the programs I do online all the time there's a real distrust and a fear that there's insider and you know privilege and that people are benefiting off of the government and it's it's not good for the system. So I could I could see why that would be a big concern. Interestingly the person who's not communicating just moved the markets by coming out and being really hawkish. He used the the sort of pulpit of the Fed. Um, do we are we looking at a more hawkish Fed now? Like did did pe did people get that wrong thinking that with a new Fed chair was going to be a much more dovish situation? >> We we have a new Fed chair. We also had some big changes in inflation this year. And I I think what's driving the what the market saw as a hawkish turn at the Fed, I think is really a reflection of what's happened in the economy. So again, to have inflation hit 4% in May, we are now a full 5 years of not making it to 2% inflation. And also, we got a lot of good news in recent months on the labor market. So in terms of the Fed's dual mandate, it's maximum employment, it's price stability, maximum employment largely looks like it's in a pretty good place, and price stability is not is going in the wrong direction. And it's been a really vexing part like they just really haven't been able to, you know, get that all the way back uh to target. And so I think you're seeing from the committee and what Marcus really reacted to who was the dot plot. >> So 18 Fed officials, Kevin Wars did not participate. So he actually is not in this. 18 Fed officials, they go through an exercise of essentially it's like what do you think it's going to take to get us back to price stability, maximum employment over the next few years? And uh nine of those Fed officials said they thought it would be appropriate to raise rates at some point this year. Eight said we can hold rates and one said cut. Now that was like sometime before the end of the year what they thought it that's what they kind of thought it would take to get back to get inflation back down. Now they all agreed including Worsh to keep rates steady, right? So they weren't ready to move rates. Even the people who think we probably will need to do it later in the year, they weren't ready yet. They don't have all the information they need. But to me, what this showed that dot plot is just a frustration of the commit that like inflation is still a problem and that is the problem because the labor market is looking a lot better. So to me it's the economics driving it, not Kevin War showed up and all these Fed officials hadn't been taking inflation seriously, which is not the case. Uh and now they've switched. It just was kind of a confluence of factors. Uh and and I think one thing that Wars did that probably did help focus and really get this like the Fed is serious about inflation is he greatly shortened the statement >> that came out and the last line of the statement was the committee will deliver price stability >> and he repeated that multiple times in the press conference. Now the Fed still has a maximum employment mandate. So it's not like he threw that out the window. But it was a very clear like we have a problem right now and it is inflation and we are focused on that and we are promising you we are going to get this done. >> So I do think by this was a case where probably focusing in being very direct like the message came across loud and clear. >> Uh sometimes fed speak is not loud and clear like it can like throw a lot of stuff into the hopper. >> Yeah. And so I think that that is something and he repeated that. Now, do I think Kevin Worsh is in favor of raising rates? I'm not so sure, right? Because again, he didn't put a dot down. He hasn't said that that's what it'll take. There's a lot of there's a lot of inflation right now that's kind of tied to stuff that's probably going to go away. So So I don't know exactly where he stands. And I think that will be important that the Fed chair doesn't at the end of the day, the Fed chair is one vote just like the other voting members, but the Fed chair is the leader. They ceue up the option, you know, so it is really important like what his what he thinks it's going to take to get it done. >> And he hasn't he's been pretty koi about that. He hasn't wanted to say too much. Uh so we'll we'll see. But I I think the markets were correct to say, hey, this Fed is they're they're focused on inflation. like that is the number one priority. They're committed uh to get that done. They're very divided on what it's going to take >> to get it done in terms of interest rates, but that's also going to depend on how the economy evolves over the coming months. So, some have speculated that uh with an election and you spent so much time at the Fed and you know we always go around about this like do they not want to get involved right before an election so that muddies things for them because they might want to wait but do they have to go in July because they don't want to go anywhere near the election. Does it have that much of an impact like if they see the data coming through will they act? Is it important that they do or do you think that that they are going to have to take that into consideration? the election cycle does not have an impact in their decision-making. It It's one of those things where, you know, if you get into this, well, we we can't go here because the election, oh, but if we wait, it's going to be about the like, as soon as you start having put that into the calculus, there's no there's no right answer. Like, anything the Fed does is going to be subject to a complaint that they're playing politics. You wait, you don't wait, you don't. So they very much are in the spirit of put your head down, look at the data, what are you, you know, so like they don't, it's kind of the same way. They can't get too cute about trying to get markets to do X, Y, or Z because like, you know, like it's just not um you can't get too fancy. So with the, you know, the the Fed Fed officials will say, you know, we just put our heads down. We don't, you know, we ignore the the electoral politics. And I re like I really do take them at their word. And if you look at decisions made around elections, it I think it's hard to make a case that it's like, oh, it's just about politics. And, you know, it's the like the Fed, you know, these are not elected officials. I mean the Supreme Court again affirmed that they have you know some protections you know so they're not under direct influence but they do understand anything in Washington DC is touched by politics it's all around them and again Congress at any time can change the Fed right so it's not they can't be cavalier about it but that's just where their best strategy is just to make their decisions based on the economics not I mean and they have to be able to show their work and show how it ties back to the economics as long they do that then they're just they really are better off not to play in the timing and the politics and um >> but you know someone if they do that >> yes and and they they're pretty good the the the Fed always gets blamed like they are a really good punching bag because they don't tend to punch back so it >> comes with the job uh Claudia we couldn't have thought of a better time to catch up with you with everything that's going on thank you so much for your time. It was a pleasure to have you. >> Yeah, thank you. Appreciate it.

and kind of abrupt. You know, the

sudden it contracts or move backward.

somethings have to go wrong all at once.

recession.
Hello, welcome to Wealthon. Maggie Lake.
Joining me today to discuss the outlook
for the US economy and labor market is

Consulting. Hello. Wonderful to have you
on.
>> Hi, great to be here today. Thank you.
>> Where where are you on recession? And we
have to ask you because you created one
of the benchmarks for the Fed that
people measure. Are we in a recession?
Have we had a recession? Is the US even
allowed to have a recession? So I am not
on re recession watch right now.
>> So that uh you know there there are
concerns to have about the economy. I
think there's some big
structural shifts potentially happening
in the economy. We talked about AI. We
talked about you know all of these the
cost shocks the that really can rewire
kind of the fundamentals of the economy
sometimes in a very persistent way. that
is different than a recession, which is
it's a broad-based contraction economic
activity, but they tend to be
short-lived. They're temporary. They're
not like shifts in the trend, the whole
direction where we're headed. They're
they're much more abrupt uh changes. And
and right now,
the the signs that we often have of a
recession don't appear. As I said, the
labor market has been stable. The
unemployment rate is not
>> uh rising.
uh there seems to be growth is good. It
it may not be it may be more focused on
the buildout of artificial intelligence
and the data centers but there's a lot
of capital investment happening. It's
